data.description:
# Repo-snapshot isolation for code-reading gates

## Context

The rebar MCP server is a long-lived process pinned to ONE working directory. Every
operation that reads PROJECT SOURCE CODE (the LLM gates: plan-review, completion-
verification, ticket-review, code-review, spec-scan) reads whatever branch + uncommitted
changes happen to be in that checkout at call time. This session hit the failure directly:
a completion-verifier read the wrong branch (a parallel task had switched the shared
checkout) and produced a FALSE-NEGATIVE verdict on work that was correctly merged to main.
The broader problem: an MCP server used by a DISTRIBUTED TEAM working CONCURRENTLY cannot
have all gates reading one mutable shared working tree, and an HMAC-signed verdict computed
against a moving branch is not reproducible — so the attestation is worthless.

Design grounded against mainstream practice (GitLab Gitaly, Sourcegraph gitserver/zoekt,
GitHub tarball API, Bazel, ccache, Nix, in-toto). Our core choices (read content from the
object store without a worktree; pin an immutable SHA; reaper + free-space cap) match what
those systems ship; the spike corrected our janitor primitive (drop the PID lease; use
kernel guarantees).

## What

Decouple every code-reading operation from the server's mutable working tree. Each such
operation accepts a `ref` (branch|tag|SHA, default `origin/main`) and a `source` mode:

- **attested** (default): `git fetch origin` -> resolve ref to an immutable SHA -> faithful
  lock-free materialization of that SHA into a content-addressed snapshot dir -> run gate ->
  record `verified_at_sha` on the ticket and SIGN over it. Reproducible.
- **local** (explicit opt-in): read the server's in-place checkout (possibly dirty) -> run
  gate -> NEVER sign. The escape hatch for "verify before I push"; structurally barred from
  producing a signed verdict (so the original footgun cannot recur).

Materialization is faithful (`read-tree` + `checkout-index`, NOT lossy `git archive`),
concurrency-safe (atomic dir-rename population, single-flight, delete-on-last-close), and
portable (pure-Python tar-free extraction, gettempdir/mkdtemp, flock+mkdir fallback, POSIX).

## Out of scope

- Sparse (file_impact-scoped) archiving — deferred optimization.
- Verdict memoization `(sha,gate)->verdict` — fast-follow.
- Automatic ticket->commit provenance (Option B) — tracked on REB-289 (dowdy-swear-bird).
- Diff-base (base+head) handling for code-review — code-review gets a single repo copy at a
  single ref in this epic, like every other operation.
- Serverless/Lambda lifecycle — long-lived POSIX server only (local/EC2/ECS); AWS volume
  placement + EFS/NFS flock caveat tracked on alto-fruit-punch.

## Success Criteria

Every code-reading gate verifies a client-specified, immutable, reproducible snapshot of the
project at a pinned SHA — never the server's mutable checkout — safely under concurrent use
by a distributed team; signed verdicts are reproducible and bound to `verified_at_sha`; the
unsigned local mode preserves the single-developer "verify before push" workflow.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] No code-reading gate reads the server's working tree in attested mode; all read a
      faithful snapshot materialized at the pinned SHA.
- [ ] A signed verdict records `verified_at_sha` and is reproducible (re-running against the
      same SHA yields a verdict over identical content).
- [ ] `local` source mode reads the in-place checkout and CANNOT produce a signed verdict.
- [ ] Concurrent gates on the same and different SHAs run without git-lock contention or
      cross-op corruption (no worktrees; atomic population; delete-on-last-close).
- [ ] Snapshot cache + janitor reclaim disk under pressure without disrupting in-flight reads.
- [ ] Private-repo fetch failures surface a descriptive, actionable credential error.
- [ ] Portable across macOS + Linux (no external tar; no hardcoded /tmp; flock+mkdir fallback).
- [ ] All six child stories closed; `make lint` + full test suite green.
data.title: Repo-snapshot isolation for code-reading gates (client ref, attested signing, concurrency-safe portable snapshots)
